meaning: Inform ATC when a specified condition or position is reached.
portugueseMeaning: Informar o ATC quando uma condição ou posição é atingida.
operationalMeaning: ATC uses 'report' to obtain a position or status update without issuing a new clearance.
whoUsesIt: Approach, Departure, Center, and Tower controllers; pilots read back and comply.
whenUsed: When ATC needs confirmation of altitude, fix passage, field in sight, or gear down.
whyUsed: Keeps separation and sequencing accurate when radar coverage or workload requires pilot updates.

realExamples.text: ANAC 123, report passing flight level one zero zero.
realExamples.speaker: Approach

realExamples.text: Recife Approach, ANAC 123, passing flight level one zero zero.
realExamples.speaker: Pilot

pilotResponses: Passing flight level one zero zero, ANAC 123.
icaoQuestions: What would you say when passing the altitude ATC asked you to report?
Why might Approach ask you to report field in sight on a visual approach?
commonMistakes: Saying only 'roger' without making the required report.
Reporting before the condition is actually met.
Using 'report' as a question — it is an instruction to inform ATC.
pronunciationTips: Keep a steady pace — do not rush the altitude numbers.

compareTerms.note: Roger means received; report requires you to send information back.
Wilco means you will comply; report means you will inform when done.

didYouKnow: Report clearances are common on non-radar segments and visual approaches.
captainStory: On a night approach into Recife, Approach kept spacing with 'report five miles final.' The FO called each fix — ATC never had to ask twice.
